*Reasoning:* Linearity allows us to pull $H$ inside the double summation and distribute it across each impulse term. This is the mathematical justification for treating the image as a linear combination of elementary responses.

**Step 4 — Define the impulse response of the degradation system.**

Define $H\big[\delta(x - \alpha, y - \beta)\big] \equiv h(x, \alpha, y, \beta)$, the response of the system to an impulse at $(\alpha, \beta)$. Substituting:

$$H\big[f(x, y)\big] = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, h(x, \alpha, y, \beta)$$

**Step 5 — Invoke spatial (position) invariance.**

Spatial invariance means the system's response to a *shifted* impulse equals the *shifted* response to the original impulse:

$$h(x, \alpha, y, \beta) = h(x - \alpha, y - \beta)$$

*Reasoning:* A point source shifted by $(\alpha, \beta)$ produces an identical blur pattern, but centered at the new location. This is the defining property of a position-invariant (or shift-invariant) system.

**Step 6 — Substitute to obtain the convolution form.**

$$H\big[f(x, y)\big] = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, h(x - \alpha, y - \beta)$$

By the definition of 2-D discrete convolution, the right-hand side is precisely $f(x, y) \ast h(x, y)$. Therefore:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) \ast h(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

**Step 7 — Take the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of both sides.**

Using the linearity of the DFT and the **Convolution Theorem** ($\mathcal{F}\{f \ast h\} = F \cdot H$):

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{f(x,y) \ast h(x,y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\}$$

$$G(u, v) = F(u, v) H(u, v) + N(u, v)$$

*Reasoning:* The Convolution Theorem is a cornerstone of linear systems theory: convolution in the spatial domain is equivalent to point-wise multiplication in the frequency domain. This is computationally advantageous because the DFT can be implemented in $O(MN \log MN)$ time using the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), compared to $O(M^2 N^2)$ for direct convolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example — Convolution & DFT:** Consider a $4 \times 4$ image $f(x, y)$ and a $3 \times 3$ blur kernel $h(x, y)$. Direct convolution requires $\approx 9 \times 16 = 144$ multiply-accumulate operations. Using the FFT approach: two forward FFTs ($4^2 \log 4^2 = 256$ ops) + one point-wise multiply ($16$ ops) + one inverse FFT ($256$ ops) $\approx 528$ ops. For larger images (e.g., $512 \times 512$), the FFT method is orders of magnitude faster.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Noise PDF Mean and Variance

This is a **frequently tested KTU table**. The following computations yield the mean and variance of each noise distribution:

| Noise Type | Mean $\bar{\eta}$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Gaussian | $\bar{\eta} = 0$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| Rayleigh | $\bar{\eta} = a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{b(4 - \pi)}{4}$ |
| Erlang (Gamma) | $\bar{\eta} = \dfrac{b}{a}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{b}{a^2}$ |
| Exponential | $\bar{\eta} = \dfrac{1}{a}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{a^2}$ |
| Uniform | $\bar{\eta} = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$ |
| Impulse (S&P) | $\bar{\eta} = (P_s + P_p)\cdot\text{const}$ | — (depends on $P_s, P_p$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Erlang PDF is sometimes called the **Gamma PDF** in alternate textbooks. The KTU syllabus uses the term **"Erlang distribution"** with the constraint that $b$ (the shape parameter) must be a *positive integer*. The Exponential distribution is simply the Erlang with $b = 1$, and this simplification appears in past KTU question papers.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Uniform Noise Mean and Variance (Worked-Out)

A common KTU Part A question: *"Derive the mean and variance of the uniform PDF."*

**Step 1 — Write the PDF.**

$$p(\eta) = \dfrac{1}{b - a}, \quad \text{for } a \leq \eta \leq b$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean.**

$$\bar{\eta} = \int_a^b \eta \cdot p(\eta)\, d\eta = \dfrac{1}{b - a} \int_a^b \eta\, d\eta = \dfrac{1}{b - a} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\eta^2}{2}\right]_a^b = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E[\eta^2]$ (the second moment).**

$$E[\eta^2] = \dfrac{1}{b - a} \int_a^b \eta^2\, d\eta = \dfrac{1}{b - a} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\eta^3}{3}\right]_a^b = \dfrac{a^2 + ab + b^2}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance.**

$$\sigma^2 = E[\eta^2] - (E[\eta])^2 = \dfrac{a^2 + ab + b^2}{3} - \left(\dfrac{a + b}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The intermediate algebraic expansion in Step 4 requires careful expansion of $\left(\dfrac{a+b}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{a^2 + 2ab + b^2}{4}$. Then, finding a common denominator of 12: $\dfrac{4(a^2 + ab + b^2) - 3(a^2 + 2ab + b^2)}{12} = \dfrac{a^2 - 2ab + b^2}{12} = \dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$. Examiners award **full marks** only when this algebraic expansion is shown explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The End-to-End Degradation / Restoration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Original Image f of x,y] --> proc1[H Degradation Operator]
    proc1 --> add1((+))
    input2[Additive Noise eta of x,y] --> add1
    add1 --> output1[Degraded Image g of x,y]
    output1 --> proc2[Restoration Filter R]
    proc2 --> output2[Estimated Image f_hat of x,y]

    subgraph FREQ[Frequency Domain Equivalence]
        F_in[F of u,v] --> mul1((X))
        H_op[H of u,v] --> mul1
        mul1 --> add2((+))
        N_in[N of u,v] --> add2
        add2 --> G_out[G of u,v]
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Tree: Taxonomy of Noise Models (Six Canonical PDFs)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Additive Noise eta of x,y] --> nodeB[Gaussian]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Rayleigh]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Erlang Gamma]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Exponential]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Uniform]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Impulse Salt and Pepper]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Sensor thermal noise]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Range imaging]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Laser radar]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[X ray imaging]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Quantization dithering]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Faulty switch]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Role of the Degradation Model in Restoration

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant DIPEngine as DIP Restoration Engine
    participant NoiseEst as Noise Estimator
    participant PSFEst as PSF Estimator
    participant Filter as Inverse Filter

    User->>DIPEngine: Input degraded image g of x,y
    DIPEngine->>NoiseEst: Extract homogeneous patch
    NoiseEst-->>DIPEngine: Return noise parameters mean and variance
    DIPEngine->>PSFEst: Estimate degradation h of x,y
    PSFEst-->>DIPEngine: Return PSF
    DIPEngine->>Filter: Compute F_hat equals G divided by H
    Filter-->>User: Return restored image f_hat of x,y
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Frequency-Domain vs Spatial-Domain Restoration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPATIAL[Spatial Domain]
        A1[g of x,y] --> A2[Deconvolve with h]
        A2 --> A3[Subtract noise estimate]
        A3 --> A4[f_hat of x,y]
    end

    subgraph FREQ[Frequency Domain]
        B1[G of u,v] --> B2[Divide by H of u,v]
        B2 --> B3[Apply Wiener filter]
        B3 --> B4[F_hat of u,v]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The two parallel pipelines illustrate the dual nature of the degradation model. The spatial-domain path uses **direct convolution and subtraction**, while the frequency-domain path exploits the elegant **point-wise division** of DFTs. The latter is computationally efficient (FFT-based) but suffers from numerical instability when $H(u,v) \to 0$ at high frequencies — this is precisely why **Wiener filtering** is later introduced as a regularized solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the image degradation model. What are its principal components, and what does each term represent physically?*

**Model Answer:**

The image degradation model is a mathematical representation of how an ideal image becomes corrupted during acquisition, transmission, or display. In its general form, it is given by:

$$g(x, y) = H\big[f(x, y)\big] + \eta(x, y)$$

The three principal components are:

- **$f(x, y)$ — Original image:** The pristine, undegraded input image that we wish to recover.
- **$H[\cdot]$ — Degradation operator:** A linear, position-invariant system (often characterized by its Point Spread Function $h(x,y)$) that models systematic distortions such as defocus blur, motion blur, or atmospheric turbulence.
- **$\eta(x, y)$ — Additive noise:** A stochastic perturbation representing random sensor noise, quantization errors, or channel interference, typically characterized by a probability density function (PDF).

The output $g(x,y)$ is the observed (degraded) image, which is the only quantity available to the restoration algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Structuring Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you **explicitly state the equation**, **name all three components**, and **give a one-line physical interpretation** of each.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]**
*List the six principal noise probability density functions used in the KTU DIP syllabus. Give the PDF expression for any two of them.*

**Model Answer:**

The six principal noise PDFs are: **(1)** Gaussian, **(2)** Rayleigh, **(3)** Erlang (Gamma), **(4)** Exponential, **(5)** Uniform, and **(6)** Impulse (Salt-and-Pepper).

**(i) Gaussian PDF:**

$$p(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\eta^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

This models electronic sensor noise and thermal noise. The mean is **0** and the variance is $\sigma^2$.

**(ii) Rayleigh PDF:**

$$p(\eta) = \frac{2}{b}(\eta - a) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\eta - a)^2}{b}\right), \quad \eta \geq a$$

This models range-imaging noise. The mean is $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ and the variance is $\dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE** of two choices (OR pattern).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 Model

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the general image degradation model $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ starting from $g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$. Clearly state the assumptions used at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the Gaussian, Rayleigh, and Erlang noise models, write the probability density function, the expression for the mean, and the expression for the variance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

*Step 1 — General model:*

$$g(x, y) = H\big[f(x, y)\big] + \eta(x, y)$$

*Step 2 — Impulse decomposition:* Represent $f(x,y)$ as a sum of weighted impulses:

$$f(x, y) = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, \delta(x - \alpha, y - \beta)$$

*Step 3 — Apply $H$ (Linearity assumed):*

$$H\big[f(x, y)\big] = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, H\big[\delta(x - \alpha, y - \beta)\big]$$

*Step 4 — Define impulse response:* Let $h(x, \alpha, y, \beta) = H[\delta(x - \alpha, y - \beta)]$.

$$H\big[f(x, y)\big] = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, h(x, \alpha, y, \beta)$$

*Step 5 — Spatial invariance:* $h(x, \alpha, y, \beta) = h(x - \alpha, y - \beta)$.

*Step 6 — Convolution form:*

$$g(x, y) = \sum_{\alpha} \sum_{\beta} f(\alpha, \beta)\, h(x - \alpha, y - \beta) + \eta(x, y) = f(x, y) \ast h(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

**(b) Noise PDFs, Means, Variances:**

| Noise | PDF | Mean | Variance |
|-------|-----|------|----------|
| Gaussian | $p(\eta) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-\eta^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $0$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| Rayleigh | $p(\eta) = \dfrac{2}{b}(\eta - a) e^{-(\eta - a)^2/b}$ | $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ | $\dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$ |
| Erlang | $p(\eta) = \dfrac{a^b \eta^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} e^{-a\eta}$ | $\dfrac{b}{a}$ | $\dfrac{b}{a^2}$ |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the importance of the assumption of linearity and spatial invariance in deriving the degradation model. State the frequency-domain representation of the model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A noise patch extracted from a degraded image has pixel values $\{120, 122, 119, 121, 120, 118, 121, 119, 120, 122\}$ with mean $\bar{\eta} = 120.2$. Assuming **uniform noise** on a range $[a, b]$, estimate the parameters $a$ and $b$ from the sample mean and variance. Assume the standard deviation of the sample is $\sigma_\eta \approx 1.4$ intensity levels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Linearity guarantees that the principle of superposition holds, allowing us to express the image as a sum of impulses and apply $H$ to each independently. Spatial (position) invariance means the PSF is the same at every pixel location. Together, these two assumptions reduce the general (non-linear, non-stationary) model to a tractable 2-D convolution.

In the **frequency domain**, applying the 2-D DFT and the Convolution Theorem gives:

$$G(u, v) = H(u, v) F(u, v) + N(u, v)$$

This form is computationally efficient (via FFT) and forms the basis of Wiener filtering, constrained least-squares restoration, and the inverse filter — all of which are downstream topics in the syllabus.

**(b) Parameter estimation for uniform noise:**

*Step 1 — Use the mean formula:*

$$\bar{\eta} = \frac{a + b}{2} \implies a + b = 2 \bar{\eta} = 2 \times 120.2 = 240.4$$

*Step 2 — Use the variance formula:*

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(b-a)^2}{12} \implies b - a = \sqrt{12 \sigma^2} = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot \sigma$$

Given $\sigma \approx 1.4$:

$$b - a = 2\sqrt{3} \times 1.4 \approx 2 \times 1.732 \times 1.4 \approx 4.85$$

*Step 3 — Solve the linear system:*

$$a = \frac{240.4 - 4.85}{2} \approx 117.78$$

$$b = \frac{240.4 + 4.85}{2} \approx 122.63$$

Therefore, the estimated uniform noise parameters are $a \approx 117.78$ and $b \approx 122.63$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Mark-Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Failing to state assumptions** (linearity + spatial invariance) in Part (a) costs **at least 2 marks**. Always say *"under the assumptions of linearity and spatial invariance"* before deriving.
> 2. **Mixing up Euler's constant $e$ with the noise parameter $a$** in Erlang PDF is a recurrent error. Remember: $a$ is the *rate parameter* (positive real), not the base of natural logarithms.
> 3. **Confusing the convolution operator $\ast$ with multiplication** in the spatial-domain equation leads to immediate mark loss in any derivation sub-part.
> 4. **Not stating units** for noise parameters (intensity levels, not pixels) is a common 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 5. In computational Part (b) questions, students often **forget to take the square root** of $12 \sigma^2$ when computing $b - a$. Show every algebraic step explicitly to recover partial marks even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The general degradation model** is $g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$, and the LSI form is $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$.
- **Frequency-domain form:** $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$, derived via the **Convolution Theorem**.
- **Two key assumptions:** **(1)** Linearity, **(2)** Spatial (position) invariance. Without these, the model is non-convolutional and intractable for the standard restoration pipeline.
- **Six noise PDFs:** Gaussian, Rayleigh, Erlang, Exponential, Uniform, Impulse (S&P). Exponential is a special case of Erlang with shape parameter $b = 1$.
- **Mean and variance formulas** for all six PDFs are **high-frequency KTU exam items** — memorize the table in Section 2.2.
- **Noise parameter estimation** is performed on a homogeneous image patch using **sample mean** $\bar{\eta}$ and **sample variance** $\sigma^2_\eta$ formulas.
- **Periodic noise** appears as discrete spikes in $N(u,v)$ and is best filtered in the frequency domain using **notch reject filters**.
- **SNR** quantifies the relative strength of signal vs. noise and is reported in **decibels (dB)**.
- The model $H$ is the **bridge to restoration**: an accurately estimated $H$ and characterized noise $\eta$ enable Wiener filtering, inverse filtering, and constrained least-squares restoration in Module 3's later sections.
- **Practical insight:** A homogeneous background region of the corrupted image (e.g., a blank wall) is the *gold standard* patch for noise parameter estimation in real engineering workflows.
- **Compute tip:** Always pad images with zeros (or use a circular wraparound) before computing FFTs to avoid border artifacts in convolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unconstraint Restoration and Constraint Restoration. Inverse Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT755 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unconstrained Restoration and Constrained Restoration. Inverse Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image Degradation and Restoration Model

A digital image, during acquisition, transmission, or storage, undergoes **degradation** caused by sensor noise, motion blur, atmospheric turbulence, or poor optics. **Image restoration** is the process of reconstructing or estimating the original, undegraded image from its degraded observation using *a priori* knowledge of the degradation phenomenon.

The standard linear, position-invariant (space-invariant) degradation model is expressed in the spatial domain as:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

and equivalently in the frequency domain as:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

Where:
- $f(x,y)$ — original (true) image
- $g(x,y)$ — observed/degraded image
- $h(x,y)$ — degradation (Point Spread Function, PSF)
- $\eta(x,y)$ — additive noise
- $H(u,v), G(u,v), F(u,v), N(u,v)$ — their 2-D Fourier transforms
- $*$ — 2-D convolution operator
- The matrix $H$ in the spatial formulation represents the linear operator derived from $h(x,y)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Restoration methods are classified into two broad paradigms based on the optimization criterion used — **Unconstrained Restoration** (no constraints imposed on the estimate) and **Constrained Restoration** (the estimate must satisfy known physical/statistical constraints).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Dusty Windowpane" Analogy**

Imagine you are trying to view a beautiful landscape (the **true image** $f$) through a **dusty windowpane** (the **degradation** $h$) while there is also **rain on the glass** (the **noise** $\eta$). What you actually see is $g$, the corrupted view.

- **Inverse Filtering** is like trying to *undo* the dust mathematically by directly dividing the observed image by the dust's transfer function. It works beautifully when the dust is mild, but it catastrophically *amplifies the raindrops* wherever the window is nearly transparent (i.e., where $H(u,v) \approx 0$).
- **Unconstrained Restoration** is the same as inverse filtering in the basic least-squares sense — it is the simplest estimate with no rules attached.
- **Constrained Restoration** is the smart variant — you impose an extra rule (e.g., "the restored image must be **smooth**") to prevent the noise from blowing up the answer. This is the **Wiener-style** family of filters.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU-Standard Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Restoration** | The reconstruction of an image from its degraded version, using knowledge of the degradation function. |
| **Unconstrained Restoration** | Estimation strategy that finds $\hat{f}$ by minimizing the norm of the residual $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2$ *without* any auxiliary constraint on $\hat{f}$. |
| **Constrained Restoration** | Estimation strategy that minimizes a *criterion* (e.g., $\Vert Q\hat{f} \Vert^2$) subject to a *constraint* (e.g., $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$). |
| **Inverse Filter** | The transfer function $H^{-1}(u,v)$ such that $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$, yielding the original spectrum where $H$ is non-zero. |
| **PSF (Point Spread Function)** | The impulse response $h(x,y)$ of the degradation system. |
| **MTF (Modulation Transfer Function)** | $\vert H(u,v) \vert$, the magnitude of the optical transfer function. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Contrast:** Inverse filtering is the *theoretical* optimum in the *no-noise* case. Constrained restoration is the *practical* optimum when noise is present.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degradation Model in Matrix Form

For digital images of size $M \times N$ sampled into lexicographic (column-stacked) vectors of length $MN$, the convolution is expressed as a matrix multiplication:

$$g = H f + \eta$$

Where $H$ is a large (in general, $MN \times MN$) **block-circulant** matrix constructed from the PSF. Block-circulant matrices are diagonalized by the 2-D DFT, which is what permits the elegant frequency-domain solutions.

---

## 2.2 Unconstrained Restoration — Theory

The goal is to find an estimate $\hat{f}$ that makes the residual $g - H\hat{f}$ as small as possible in the Euclidean (least-squares) sense.

**Criterion:**

$$\hat{f} = \underset{f}{\arg\min} \; J(\hat{f}) = \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2$$

Setting the gradient w.r.t. $\hat{f}$ to zero (assuming real, symmetric $H$):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{f}} = -2 H^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\hat{f} = (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} g}$$

When $H$ is square and invertible (i.e., PSF is non-degenerate), this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\hat{f} = H^{-1} g}$$

In the frequency domain, using the diagonalization of block-circulant matrices by the DFT:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}$$

This **is** inverse filtering.

---

## 2.3 Constrained Restoration — Theory

The unconstrained estimate can be catastrophically unstable because the inverse $H^{-1}$ amplifies noise wherever $H(u,v)$ is small. A **constraint** is introduced to regularize the problem.

**The standard constrained criterion (Hunt's formulation):**

$$\text{Minimize} \; \Vert Q \hat{f} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$$

Here $Q$ is a **linear operator** (typically a high-pass operator like a Laplacian) chosen to enforce *smoothness* on the solution — penalizing excessive high-frequency content in $\hat{f}$.

Using the **method of Lagrange multipliers** with multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\hat{f}, \lambda) = \Vert Q\hat{f} \Vert^2 + \lambda \left( \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2 - \Vert \eta \Vert^2 \right)$$

Differentiating and setting the gradient to zero:

$$2 Q^{T}Q \hat{f} - 2 \lambda H^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) = 0$$

$$(H^{T} H + \gamma Q^{T} Q) \hat{f} = H^{T} g, \quad \text{where} \; \gamma = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

The closed-form solution in the **frequency domain** is:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^{2} + \gamma \vert P(u,v) \vert^{2}} \, G(u,v)}$$

Where $P(u,v)$ is the DFT of the operator $Q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The denominator $\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \gamma \vert P(u,v) \vert^2$ is **never zero** for $\gamma > 0$, which is precisely why the constrained solution is numerically stable while the pure inverse filter blows up.

When $\gamma = 0$, the constrained formula collapses to the unconstrained inverse filter. When $\gamma = 1$ and $Q$ is chosen appropriately (e.g., identity), one recovers the **Wiener filter** form. This is why constrained restoration is sometimes called the *generalized Wiener* or *regularized* restoration.

---

## 2.4 Inverse Filtering — Theory

The inverse filter is the direct frequency-domain reciprocal of the degradation function. It is the *naive* solution that ignores noise.

**Filter form:**

$$H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)} \quad \text{(formal)}$$ 

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$$

**Substituting** $G = HF + N$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v)F(u,v)}{H(u,v)} + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The **error term** $\dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ is the critical problem — wherever $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ is small, the noise is amplified *enormously*.

**Practical modifications of inverse filtering:**

1. **Radius-limited inverse filter:** Apply the inverse only within a radius $R$ around the origin (low frequencies where MTF is usually large), zero out the rest.
   $$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}, & D(u,v) \le R \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
2. **Energy-limited inverse filter:** Compute the cumulative energy ratio of $H$ and threshold.
3. **Use of pseudo-inverse:** $H_{\text{inv}} = H^* / (\vert H \vert^2 + \epsilon)$ for a small constant $\epsilon > 0$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Degradation (Spatial) | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Convolution + additive noise |
| Degradation (Frequency) | $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | DFT-based |
| Unconstrained (Spatial) | $\hat{f} = (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} g$ | Matrix form |
| Unconstrained (Frequency) | $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$ | = Inverse filter |
| Constrained (Frequency) | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{*} G}{\vert H \vert^{2} + \gamma \vert P \vert^{2}}$ | $\gamma = 1/\lambda$ |
| Constrained Error | $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^{2} = \Vert \eta \Vert^{2}$ | Lagrange constraint |
| Error term in Inverse | $\hat{F} = F + N/H$ | Noise amplification term |
| Energy ratio test | $\dfrac{\sum_{u,v \in D} \vert H \vert^{2}}{\sum_{\text{all}} \vert H \vert^{2}} \le T$ | $T$ is threshold |
| Noise spectrum estimate | $\hat{N}(u,v) = G(u,v) - H(u,v)\hat{F}_{LP}(u,v)$ | From low-pass estimate |
| Pseudo-inverse | $H_{\text{inv}} \approx H^{*}/(\vert H \vert^{2} + \epsilon)$ | $\epsilon$ tiny constant |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** Patient motion and finite-aperture PSFs blur scans; constrained least-squares restoration deblurs while suppressing amplifier noise.
- **Satellite Remote Sensing:** Atmospheric turbulence and satellite jitter are modeled with known (or estimated) PSFs; inverse/constrained filters sharpen the imagery for cartography.
- **Astronomy:** Hubble Space Telescope's pre-COSTAR images had a known spherical-aberration PSF; deconvolution restored optical quality.
- **Forensics & Surveillance:** Motion-blur deblurring of license plates and faces.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Restoring images affected by out-of-focus optics on assembly lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unconstrained Restoration (Matrix Form)

**Starting Criterion:** Find $\hat{f}$ minimizing

$$J(\hat{f}) = \Vert g - H \hat{f} \Vert^{2} = (g - H\hat{f})^{T}(g - H\hat{f})$$

**Step 1:** Expand the squared norm.

$$J(\hat{f}) = g^{T} g - g^{T} H \hat{f} - \hat{f}^{T} H^{T} g + \hat{f}^{T} H^{T} H \hat{f}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\hat{f}$ using matrix calculus identity $\dfrac{\partial(x^{T}Ax)}{\partial x} = 2Ax$ and $\dfrac{\partial(b^{T}x)}{\partial x} = b$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{f}} = -2 H^{T} g + 2 H^{T} H \hat{f}$$

**Step 3:** Set the gradient to zero for the minimum.

$$- H^{T} g + H^{T} H \hat{f} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\hat{f}$ (assuming $(H^{T}H)$ is invertible).

$$\boxed{\hat{f} = (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} g}$$

**Step 5:** For the *ideal* case where $H$ is square and non-singular:

$$(H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T} = H^{-1}(H^{T})^{-1}H^{T} = H^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{f} = H^{-1} g$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Unconstrained in the Frequency Domain

**Step 1:** Apply the 2-D DFT to the matrix equation $\hat{f} = H^{-1} g$. Because $H$ is block-circulant, $H^{-1}$ is also block-circulant and diagonalized by the DFT matrix $W$.

$$W \hat{f} = W H^{-1} W^{-1} W g$$

**Step 2:** Identify the diagonal elements of $W H^{-1} W^{-1}$. They are exactly $1/H(u,v)$ (the eigenvalues of $H^{-1}$ are reciprocals of those of $H$, and the eigenvalues of $H$ are the DFT samples $H(u,v)$).

**Step 3:** Therefore, element-wise:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Critical observation:** The reconstruction error is $E(u,v) = N(u,v)/H(u,v)$. If $H(u,v) = 0$ at any frequency, the estimate is **undefined** (or $\pm \infty$); if $H(u,v)$ is merely small, the noise is **massively amplified**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Constrained Restoration (Hunt / Lagrange)

**Starting constrained problem:**

$$\min_{\hat{f}} \Vert Q \hat{f} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \Vert g - H \hat{f} \Vert^{2} = \Vert \eta \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 1:** Form the Lagrangian functional with multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\hat{f}, \lambda) = \Vert Q\hat{f} \Vert^{2} + \lambda \left( \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^{2} - \Vert \eta \Vert^{2} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Expand.

$$\mathcal{L} = \hat{f}^{T} Q^{T} Q \hat{f} + \lambda \left( \hat{f}^{T} H^{T} H \hat{f} - 2 g^{T} H \hat{f} + g^{T} g - \eta^{T} \eta \right)$$

**Step 3:** Take gradient w.r.t. $\hat{f}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{f}} = 2 Q^{T} Q \hat{f} + 2 \lambda H^{T} H \hat{f} - 2 \lambda H^{T} g = 0$$

**Step 4:** Group terms in $\hat{f}$.

$$\left( H^{T} H + \gamma Q^{T} Q \right) \hat{f} = H^{T} g, \quad \text{where} \; \gamma \equiv \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\hat{f}$.

$$\hat{f} = \left( H^{T} H + \gamma Q^{T} Q \right)^{-1} H^{T} g$$

**Step 6:** Diagonalize via DFT. The matrix $H^{T}H$ becomes $\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 \cdot I$, $Q^{T}Q$ becomes $\vert P(u,v) \vert^2 \cdot I$, and $H^{T}$ becomes $H^{*}(u,v) \cdot I$. This yields the closed-form frequency-domain estimate:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^{2} + \gamma \vert P(u,v) \vert^{2}} \, G(u,v)}$$

**Step 7 (Sanity checks):**
- If $\gamma = 0$: $\hat{F} = H^{*}G / \vert H \vert^2 = G/H$ → reduces to **inverse filter**.
- If $\gamma \to \infty$: $\hat{F} \to 0$ → estimate is suppressed (over-regularized).
- $Q$ commonly taken as the **Laplacian** $\nabla^2$ (for smoothness) so that $P(u,v) = -4\pi^2(u^2 + v^2)$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — 1-D Conceptual Restoration

**Given:** A 1-D signal with degradation function $H(u) = 0.5$ (constant) and observed $G(u) = 1.0 + N(u)$ where $N(u)$ is white noise with $\vert N(u) \vert^2 = 0.01$ uniformly.

**Unconstrained (Inverse) estimate:**

$$\hat{F}(u) = \frac{G(u)}{H(u)} = \frac{1.0 + N(u)}{0.5} = 2.0 + \frac{N(u)}{0.5} = 2.0 + 2N(u)$$

Noise variance is amplified by a factor of $1/0.5^2 = 4$.

**Constrained estimate** with $\gamma \vert P(u) \vert^2 = 0.05$:

$$\hat{F}(u) = \frac{H^{*} G}{\vert H \vert^2 + \gamma \vert P \vert^2} = \frac{0.5 (1.0 + N)}{0.25 + 0.05} = \frac{0.5(1.0 + N)}{0.3} = 1.667 + 1.667 N$$

Noise variance amplified by only $(1.667/0.5)^2 \cdot (0.01/0.01) = 11.11$ — wait, this seems wrong; the *denominator* increased so the *transfer of signal* dropped too. Let us recompute the noise gain ratio:

$$\frac{\text{Noise output}}{\text{Noise input}} = \frac{H^{*}}{\vert H \vert^{2} + \gamma \vert P \vert^{2}} = \frac{0.5}{0.30} \approx 1.667$$

vs inverse filter noise gain $= 1/H = 2.0$. The constrained filter has **lower** noise gain, at the cost of slight signal attenuation. **This is the regularization trade-off.**

---

## 3.5 Selection of $\gamma$ (KTU-Critical Topic)

The Lagrange multiplier $\gamma$ is **not** a free parameter — it is determined by the constraint equation. In practice, $\gamma$ is chosen such that the residual matches the noise energy:

$$\Vert g - H \hat{f}(\gamma) \Vert^{2} = \Vert \eta \Vert^{2}$$

**Iterative procedure:**

1. Initialize $\gamma = 0$.
2. Compute $\hat{f}(\gamma)$ from the closed-form.
3. Compute residual $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2$.
4. If residual $> \Vert \eta \Vert^2$, increase $\gamma$; else decrease.
5. Repeat until convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\Vert \eta \Vert^2$ is unknown, it is approximated from a low-pass estimate of the image (because high frequencies are dominated by noise).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Degradation/Restoration Process

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the full signal path from the true image through degradation, observation, and finally through the two restoration paradigms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Image f x,y] --> B[Degradation PSF h x,y]
    B --> C[Convolution Operation]
    D[Additive Noise eta x,y] --> C
    C --> E[Observed Degraded Image g x,y]
    E --> F{Restoration Paradigm}
    F -- Unconstrained --> G[Inverse Filter F = G / H]
    F -- Constrained --> H[Regularized Filter F = H* G divided by |H|^2 + gamma|P|^2]
    G --> I[Estimated Image f-hat]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Quality Evaluation PSNR SSIM MSE]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Restoration Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Degraded Image Available] --> S1{Is PSF H known?}
    S1 -- No --> S2[Blind Deconvolution Path: Estimate PSF first]
    S1 -- Yes --> S3{Is noise negligible?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use Unconstrained: Pure Inverse Filter]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Is noise variance known?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Use Constrained with gamma derived from noise]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Estimate noise from flat region; iterate gamma]
    S6 --> S8[Apply regularized inverse filter]
    S7 --> S8
    S4 --> S9[Apply inverse filter with radius or energy cutoff]
    S8 --> S10[Post-processing: edge enhancement, denoising]
    S9 --> S10
    S2 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Final Restored Output]
```

## 4.3 Mathematical Pipeline of Constrained Restoration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: g x,y] --> A2[2-D DFT to get G u,v]
    A2 --> A3[Estimate or Supply H u,v]
    A2 --> A4[Estimate or Supply Noise Spectrum N u,v]
    A3 --> A5[Compute H conjugate u,v]
    A3 --> A6[Compute modulus squared of H u,v]
    A4 --> A7[Determine gamma via noise energy]
    A7 --> A8[Build Laplacian operator P u,v]
    A8 --> A9[Compute gamma times modulus squared of P u,v]
    A5 --> A10[Numerator: H* times G]
    A6 --> A11[Denominator: |H|^2 + gamma |P|^2]
    A9 --> A11
    A10 --> A12[Element-wise Division]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Inverse 2-D DFT]
    A13 --> A14[Output: Restored f-hat x,y]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Inverse vs Constrained

| Aspect | Pure Inverse Filter | Constrained Restoration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Noise handling | None (catastrophic amplification) | Regularized via $\gamma$ term |
| Stability | Unstable for small $\vert H \vert$ | Numerically stable |
| Computational cost | Lowest (one division) | Moderate (one division per iteration of $\gamma$) |
| Prior knowledge needed | Only $H(u,v)$ | $H(u,v)$, $Q$ (e.g., Laplacian), $\Vert \eta \Vert^2$ |
| Best use case | High SNR, well-conditioned $H$ | Real-world noisy images |
| Limitation | Blows up at zeros of $H$ | Requires tuning of $\gamma$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define image degradation model. State the assumptions made in the linear space-invariant degradation model.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]

**Model Answer:**

The image degradation model is a mathematical description of how a clean image is corrupted during acquisition, transmission, or storage. In the linear, position-invariant (shift-invariant) form, it is given by:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Equivalently in the frequency domain:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Assumptions:**
1. **Linearity** — the degradation operator obeys superposition and scaling.
2. **Space (Position) Invariance** — $h(x,y)$ does not change with spatial location; the same PSF blurs the entire image.
3. **Additive noise** — corruption is modeled as a zero-mean noise term added after blurring.
4. The noise $\eta$ is often assumed **statistically independent** of $f$.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between unconstrained and constrained image restoration.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Unconstrained Restoration | Constrained Restoration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Objective | Minimize $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2$ | Minimize $\Vert Q\hat{f} \Vert^2$ subject to $\Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$ |
| Auxiliary rule on $\hat{f}$ | None | Yes (smoothness via $Q$, typically Laplacian) |
| Noise sensitivity | Very high | Controlled via $\gamma$ |
| Stability | Unstable at zeros of $H$ | Stable (denominator is always positive) |
| Form | $\hat{F} = G/H$ | $\hat{F} = \dfrac{H^{*}G}{\vert H\vert^2 + \gamma\vert P\vert^2}$ |
| Use | Theoretical / noise-free | Practical / noisy images |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Derive the expression for the unconstrained restoration filter in the frequency domain. Why is it called an "inverse filter"? What is its fundamental limitation? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the criterion.**

Find $\hat{f}$ minimizing

$$J(\hat{f}) = \Vert g - H\hat{f} \Vert^2 = (g - H\hat{f})^{T}(g - H\hat{f})$$

**Step 2 — Expand.**

$$J = g^{T}g - g^{T}H\hat{f} - \hat{f}^{T}H^{T}g + \hat{f}^{T}H^{T}H\hat{f}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate.**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{f}} = -2H^{T}g + 2H^{T}H\hat{f}$$

**Step 4 — Set to zero.**

$$H^{T}H\hat{f} = H^{T}g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{f} = (H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T}g$$

For non-singular $H$: $\hat{f} = H^{-1}g$.

**Step 5 — Transform to frequency domain.** Because $H$ is block-circulant, it is diagonalized by the 2-D DFT:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 6 — Why "inverse filter"?** The frequency response of the restoration filter is $1/H(u,v)$ — the multiplicative inverse of the degradation transfer function. It "inverts" the blurring process.

**Step 7 — Limitation.** Substituting $G = HF + N$:

$$\hat{F} = F + \frac{N}{H}$$

The noise is amplified by $1/\vert H \vert$. Where $\vert H \vert$ is small, the result is dominated by amplified noise. Where $H = 0$, the filter is **undefined**. This is why the inverse filter is fundamentally unsuitable for noisy images.

---

#### (b) Starting from the Lagrangian of the constrained restoration problem, derive the closed-form frequency-domain expression for the estimated image. [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constrained problem statement.**

$$\min_{\hat{f}} \Vert Q\hat{f}\Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian with multiplier $\lambda$.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\hat{f}, \lambda) = \hat{f}^{T}Q^{T}Q\hat{f} + \lambda\left[ (g - H\hat{f})^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) - \eta^{T}\eta \right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\hat{f}$ and set to zero.**

$$2Q^{T}Q\hat{f} - 2\lambda H^{T}(g - H\hat{f}) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow (H^{T}H + \gamma Q^{T}Q)\hat{f} = H^{T}g, \quad \gamma = 1/\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Solve.**

$$\hat{f} = (H^{T}H + \gamma Q^{T}Q)^{-1}H^{T}g$$

**Step 5 — Diagonalize via 2-D DFT.** $H^{T}H \to \vert H \vert^2 I$, $Q^{T}Q \to \vert P \vert^2 I$, $H^{T} \to H^{*}I$. Multiplying element-wise:

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^{2} + \gamma \vert P(u,v) \vert^{2}} \, G(u,v)}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The denominator $\vert H \vert^2 + \gamma \vert P \vert^2$ is always positive for $\gamma > 0$, ensuring stability. As $\gamma \to 0$, the formula reduces to the inverse filter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) An image is degraded by an atmospheric turbulence modeled by $H(u,v) = e^{-k(u^2+v^2)^{5/6}}$ with $k = 0.0025$. The observed image spectrum at frequency $(u,v) = (10, 5)$ has $G = 12 + 8j$ and the noise spectrum there is $N = 0.5 + 0.3j$. Compute the unconstrained restoration estimate $\hat{F}$ at this frequency. Comment on the result. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model QP Style]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $(u^2 + v^2)^{5/6}$.**

$$u^2 + v^2 = 100 + 25 = 125$$

$$(125)^{5/6} = 125^{0.8333} = e^{0.8333 \ln 125} = e^{0.8333 \times 4.8283} = e^{4.024} \approx 55.78$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H(u,v)$.**

$$H = e^{-0.0025 \times 55.78} = e^{-0.1395} \approx 0.870$$

**Step 3 — Apply inverse filter formula.**

$$\hat{F} = \frac{G}{H} = \frac{12 + 8j}{0.870} = 13.79 + 9.20j$$

**Step 4 — Compute the noise contribution.**

$$\frac{N}{H} = \frac{0.5 + 0.3j}{0.870} = 0.575 + 0.345j$$

**Step 5 — Comment.** The noise term is $\sim 4-5\%$ of the true signal contribution — still manageable. **However**, at higher frequencies (larger $u, v$), $H \to 0$ and the noise term will dominate. This illustrates exactly why the **constrained** formulation is preferred in practice.

---

#### (b) Explain the "radius-limited" and "energy-compensated" inverse filtering approaches. How do they mitigate the noise amplification issue? [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Approach 1: Radius-Limited Inverse Filter.**

Define a cutoff radius $R$ from the frequency origin. The filter is applied only inside a circle of radius $R$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}, & D(u,v) = \sqrt{u^2+v^2} \le R \\ 0, & D(u,v) > R \end{cases}$$

**Mitigation:** Since $H$ typically decreases with frequency, restricting to the inner disc avoids the most noise-sensitive region. The choice of $R$ is empirical (often $R \approx 0.5$ to $0.8 \times$ image size).

**Approach 2: Energy-Compensated Inverse Filter.**

Accumulate $\vert H(u,v) \vert^2$ from the origin outward until a threshold fraction $T$ (e.g., 95%) of total energy is enclosed. The filter is applied only within this region.

$$\text{Apply inverse} \iff \frac{\sum_{(u,v) \in \Omega} \vert H(u,v)\vert^2}{\sum_{\text{all } u,v} \vert H(u,v) \vert^2} \le T$$

**Mitigation:** The threshold $T$ ensures restoration is performed only where the PSF carries significant energy (and thus has high SNR). Outside this region, the estimate is suppressed to zero, removing the most noise-amplified components.

**Both approaches** trade off spatial resolution (some high frequencies are lost) for noise robustness — a deliberate engineering compromise.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing inverse filter with unconstrained restoration** — the inverse filter is a *special case* of unconstrained restoration (when $H$ is invertible and noise-free). Writing "inverse filter = unconstrained restoration" without qualification is **wrong**.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise term** when deriving $\hat{F}$: must explicitly show $\hat{F} = F + N/H$ to highlight the limitation.
> 3. **Writing the Lagrangian incorrectly** — the constraint equation $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$ must be inside parentheses with the multiplier $\lambda$ multiplying it, not just added.
> 4. **Mixing spatial and frequency domain** — the matrix form is spatial; the diagonalized form is frequency. Examiners deduct marks for not labeling domain clearly.
> 5. **Skipping the diagonalization step** in the constrained derivation — this is the **single most important step** (worth 2-3 marks alone) and is where many students lose points.
> 6. **Omitting the assumption** that $H$ is block-circulant / position-invariant when using DFT diagonalization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Degradation model:** $g = h * f + \eta$ spatially, $G = HF + N$ in frequency.
- **Unconstrained restoration** minimizes only $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 \Rightarrow$ closed-form $\hat{f} = (H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T}g$, which in the frequency domain **is** the inverse filter $\hat{F} = G/H$.
- **Constrained restoration** adds a smoothness penalty $\Vert Q\hat{f}\Vert^2$ with Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ (or $\gamma = 1/\lambda$), giving the regularized inverse $\hat{F} = H^{*}G / (\vert H\vert^2 + \gamma \vert P\vert^2)$.
- **Inverse filter** is the inverse of the PSF in frequency; it amplifies noise wherever $\vert H \vert$ is small.
- **Practical fixes** for inverse filter: radius-limited cutoff, energy thresholding, pseudo-inverse with $\epsilon$.
- **Selection of $\gamma$** is determined by the constraint $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$; iterative procedures are used when $\Vert \eta \Vert^2$ is unknown.
- **$Q$ is typically the Laplacian** so $P(u,v) = -4\pi^2(u^2 + v^2)$ — penalizes high-frequency "roughness" in $\hat{f}$.
- **Constrained = generalized Wiener/regularized** filter; unconstrained = pure inverse filter (special case at $\gamma = 0$).
- **Diagonalization step** (DFT of block-circulant $H$) is the **key engineering trick** that makes the problem tractable.
- **Examiners love asking:** the noise term $N/H$ in inverse filtering, the constraint equation in Lagrange derivation, and the role of $\gamma$ as a *regularization parameter* (not a Lagrange multiplier directly).
- **Always state domain** (spatial vs frequency) explicitly — a frequent KTU marking error.
- **Block-circulant assumption** is the bridge that lets us use the 2-D DFT for the matrix problem.
- The **trade-off** in constrained restoration: larger $\gamma$ → smoother output, less noise, but loss of detail; smaller $\gamma$ → sharper but noisier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weiner Filtering. Image Compression: Need for Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT755 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weiner Filtering. Image Compression: Need for Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wiener Filtering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wiener Filter (Optimal Restoration Filter)**
> The Wiener filter is the **Mean Square Error (MSE)-optimal stationary linear filter** used in image restoration. It produces an estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$ of the original undegraded image $f(x,y)$ such that the **mean square error** between the estimate and the true image is minimized.

In the frequency domain, the Wiener filter is given by:

$$
W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}\,S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)}
$$

where:
- $H^{\ast}(u,v)$ is the complex conjugate of the degradation function
- $S_{f}(u,v) = \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}$ is the **power spectral density (PSD)** of the original image
- $S_{\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}$ is the **PSD of the noise**
- $\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} = H^{\ast}(u,v)\,H(u,v)$ is the squared magnitude of the degradation function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The filter is **optimal in the MSE sense** because it minimizes $E\{\,[\,f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)\,]^{2}\,\}$, i.e., the expected squared error between the true image and the restored estimate.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Earplug"

Imagine you are listening to a song being played through a **broken speaker** in a **noisy room**. The broken speaker distorts the music (blur) and the room adds traffic noise (additive noise). A simple **inverse filter** would just "undo" the speaker distortion — but in doing so, it would *amplify the noise catastrophically* because some frequencies in the speaker response are near zero.

The **Wiener filter** acts like a **smart earplug** that:
- Reverses the speaker distortion where the speaker response is strong
- **Suppresses (dampens)** the output where the speaker response is weak and noise dominates
- Uses prior knowledge of the **noise level** and the **typical frequency content of music** to make a balanced trade-off

In this analogy:
- The broken speaker = degradation function $H(u,v)$
- The traffic noise = noise PSD $S_{\eta}(u,v)$
- The known "musical" character of the song = image PSD $S_{f}(u,v)$
- The smart earplug = Wiener filter $W(u,v)$

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just use inverse filtering?** The inverse filter $W(u,v) = 1/H(u,v)$ blows up when $H(u,v) \approx 0$. The Wiener filter is the **noise-aware** version of the inverse filter — it gracefully degrades instead of exploding.

---

## 1.3 Image Compression — Need for Compression

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Compression**
> Image compression is the art and science of representing image data using **fewer bits** while preserving the essential visual information. The **Need for Compression** arises from fundamental limits in storage capacity and transmission bandwidth.

### 1.4 Why Compression is Necessary

| Driving Factor | Quantitative Reality | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | A single 24-bit RGB $4096 \times 4096$ image = **48 MB** | High-resolution archives become unmanageable |
| **Transmission** | Uncompressed HD video at 30 fps = **1.5 Gbps** | Saturation of network channels |
| **Real-time constraints** | Video conferencing, telemedicine, remote sensing | Latency budgets are tight |
| **Hardware cost** | SSD per-GB cost, memory hierarchy | Drives economic feasibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information-Theoretic Viewpoint:** Per **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem**, the minimum average bits per symbol required to losslessly encode a source with entropy $H$ is exactly $H$ bits/symbol. Compression is the engineering realization of this bound.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — "Vacuum-Packing Clothes"

Think of image compression like **vacuum-packing a suitcase**:
- A bulky winter coat (image) takes a full drawer
- When you compress it (zip-bag, fold, roll), it occupies one-tenth the space
- When you unpack it, the coat is still functionally **identical** (lossless) or **visually indistinguishable** (lossy)
- The "air" you removed is called **redundancy** — repeated or uninformative data

The "air" in an image is the **statistical and perceptual redundancy** that can be squeezed out without losing the essential content.

### 1.6 The Three Fundamental Redundancies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** KTU explicitly identifies three types of redundancy that compression algorithms exploit.

1. **Coding Redundancy ($\eta_{c}$):** When the average code word length exceeds the source entropy. Caused by suboptimal symbol-to-codeword mapping.
2. **Inter-pixel (Spatial) Redundancy ($\eta_{s}$):** Adjacent pixels are highly correlated; one pixel is largely predictable from its neighbors.
3. **Psycho-visual Redundancy ($\eta_{pv}$):** The human visual system (HVS) cannot perceive all the information present — many bits encode imperceptible details.
</br>
</br>

**Total relative redundancy:**

$$
R_{D} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{R}}
$$

where $C_{R}$ is the **compression ratio**:

$$
C_{R} = \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}
$$

with $n_{1}$ = number of bits in the original representation and $n_{2}$ = number of bits in the compressed representation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Wiener Filter

The degraded image in the spatial domain is modelled as a **linear convolution** corrupted by **additive noise**:

$$
g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)
$$

Taking the 2D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT):

$$
G(u,v) = H(u,v)\,F(u,v) + N(u,v)
$$

### 2.2 Derivation of the Wiener Filter (Frequency Domain)

The goal: find a filter $W(u,v)$ such that

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v)
$$

is the **best linear MSE estimate** of $F(u,v)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions for the Standard Wiener Filter:**
> 1. The image and noise are **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** random processes.
> 2. The image and noise are **uncorrelated** (i.e., $E\{f(x,y)\,\eta(x,y)\} = 0$).
> 3. Both have **zero mean** (assumed for simplicity).

**Optimality condition (orthogonality principle):**

$$
E\{\,[F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)]\,G^{\ast}(u,v)\,\} = 0
$$

This leads (after substantial algebra — see SECTION 3) to the celebrated Wiener filter transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v) / S_{f}(u,v)}\,}
$$

or equivalently

$$
\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}\cdot\dfrac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v) / S_{f}(u,v)}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the formula:** The first term $1/H(u,v)$ is the inverse filter; the second fraction (always between 0 and 1) is the **noise-suppression term**. When the SNR is high (image dominates noise), the fraction $\rightarrow 1$ and the filter behaves like the inverse filter. When the SNR is low, the fraction $\rightarrow 0$ and the filter **rejects** the noisy data.

### 2.3 Special Cases and Limiting Behaviour

| Condition | Wiener Filter Behaviour | Resulting Filter |
|---|---|---|
| $\;S_{\eta}(u,v) \to 0\;$ (negligible noise) | $W \to 1/H$ | **Pure inverse filter** |
| $\;S_{f}(u,v) \to 0\;$ (no image power at $(u,v)$) | $W \to 0$ | Filter zeros out the output (no signal to recover) |
| $H(u,v) = 1$ (no blur, only noise) | $W = S_{f}/(S_{f}+S_{\eta})$ | **Pure noise-reduction filter** |
| $H(u,v) = 0$ (degradation zero at $(u,v)$) | $W = 0$ | Filter refuses to recover unobservable frequencies |

### 2.4 Practical Implementation Issues

> [!WARNING]
> The Wiener filter requires explicit (or estimated) knowledge of $S_{f}(u,v)$ and $S_{\eta}(u,v)$. In practice, only an **estimate** is available, and the filter is therefore sub-optimal. When these PSDs are **unknown**, the simplified form using a scalar $K$ is used.

**Simplified (Parameterised) Wiener Filter:**

$$
\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[\dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}\,\dfrac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}\right]\,G(u,v)
$$

where $K$ is a **user-defined noise-to-signal ratio scalar** added to make $H(u,v) = 0$ at low SNR frequencies tractable. Typical practice: $K$ is iteratively tuned until the restored image looks acceptable.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Wiener Filtering

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Degradation model | Spatial domain |
| $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Degradation in frequency domain | Frequency domain |
| $W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}/S_{f}}$ | **Wiener filter** | Unitless |
| $W(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \dfrac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}$ | **Practical Wiener filter** | Unitless |
| $e^{2} = E\{\,[\,f-\hat{f}\,]^{2}\,\}$ | Mean square error | Intensity$^2$ |
| $S_{f}(u,v) = \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}$ | Power spectral density of image | Intensity$^2$ |
| $S_{\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}$ | Power spectral density of noise | Intensity$^2$ |
| $H^{\ast}(u,v)$ | Complex conjugate of degradation | Unitless |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of Wiener Filtering

- **Astronomy:** Restoring images from the Hubble Space Telescope before corrective optics were installed.
- **Medical imaging:** Deblurring CT, MRI, and ultrasound scans corrupted by sensor noise and patient motion.
- **Forensic imaging:** Enhancing CCTV footage for license-plate recognition.
- **Remote sensing:** Cleaning satellite imagery affected by atmospheric turbulence.

---

## 2.7 Deep Theory — Why Compression is Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Understanding **redundancy types** and the **data redundancy formulation** is mandatory.

### 2.8 Source Entropy and the Information Bound

For a discrete memoryless source emitting symbols $a_{j}$ with probabilities $P(a_{j})$, the **average information per symbol** is the **entropy**:

$$
H(z) = -\sum_{j=1}^{L} P(a_{j})\, \log_{2} P(a_{j}) \quad \text{bits/symbol}
$$

The **average code-word length** for a coding scheme is:

$$
L_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{j=1}^{L} l_{j}\,P(a_{j})
$$

where $l_{j}$ is the length of the codeword for $a_{j}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's First Theorem (Noiseless Coding):** $L_{\text{avg}} \geq H(z)$ with equality achievable only for ideal coding. Any excess $L_{\text{avg}} - H(z)$ is **coding redundancy**.

### 2.9 Compression Ratio & Relative Data Redundancy

$$
C_{R} = \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}} \quad ; \quad R_{D} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{R}}
$$

- $C_{R} = 10$ means the compressed file is 10× smaller.
- $R_{D} = 0.9$ means 90% of the original data was redundant.

### 2.10 Fidelity Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> **Two approaches** to judge compressed image quality:

**Objective (Mathematical) Fidelity Criteria:**

$$
\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1}\big[f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)\big]^{2}
$$

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}
$$

$$
\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum f^{2}}{\sum (f-\hat{f})^{2}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right) \;\; \text{dB} \quad (L = \text{peak intensity})
$$

**Subjective Fidelity Criteria:** Based on **human observer ratings** (e.g., Absolute Rating Scale: Excellent, Good, Fair, Poor, Unusable).

### 2.11 Real-World Utility of Image Compression

| Domain | Compression Standard | Why Needed |
|---|---|---|
| Medical imaging (DICOM) | JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 | Reduces PACS storage from TB → GB |
| Satellite remote sensing | JPEG 2000, CCSDS | Downlink bandwidth is severely limited |
| Video streaming (Netflix, YouTube) | H.264, H.265 (HEVC), AV1 | 4K/8K streaming at < 25 Mbps |
| Document imaging | JBIG2 | Archival of legal/government records |
| Mobile photography | HEIF (HEVC in container) | Smaller files at equal visual quality |
| Scientific imaging | Lossless PNG, TIFF, FITS | Preserves exact pixel values |

### 2.12 KTU Formula Sheet — Image Compression Basics

| Quantity | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Compression ratio | $C_{R} = n_{1}/n_{2}$ | Original bits / Compressed bits |
| Relative redundancy | $R_{D} = 1 - 1/C_{R}$ | Fraction of data that is redundant |
| Source entropy | $H = -\sum P \log_{2} P$ | Lower bound on bits/symbol |
| Avg code length | $L_{\text{avg}} = \sum l_{j} P(a_{j})$ | Actual average bits/symbol |
| Coding redundancy | $\eta_{c} = L_{\text{avg}} - H$ | Wasted bits per symbol |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{MN}\sum(f-\hat{f})^{2}$ | Objective distortion measure |
| PSNR | $10\log_{10}(L^{2}/\text{MSE})$ | Peak signal-to-noise ratio (dB) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Wiener Filter

We aim to find $W(u,v)$ that minimises the mean square error:

$$
e^{2} = E\left\{\left[F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)\right]^{2}\right\}
$$

where $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v)$ is the restored image spectrum.

### Step 1 — Substitute the estimate

$$
e^{2} = E\left\{\left[F(u,v) - W(u,v)\,G(u,v)\right]^{2}\right\}
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the square

$$
e^{2} = E\left\{F(u,v)\,F^{\ast}(u,v) - W(u,v)\,G(u,v)\,F^{\ast}(u,v) - W^{\ast}(u,v)\,G^{\ast}(u,v)\,F(u,v) + \vert W(u,v)\vert^{2}\vert G(u,v)\vert^{2}\right\}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the degradation model

From $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ we have $F(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ (assuming $H \neq 0$).

Re-substituting gives:

$$
e^{2} = S_{f}(u,v) - W(u,v)\,H(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v) - W^{\ast}(u,v)\,H^{\ast}(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v) + \vert W(u,v)\vert^{2}\big[\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)\big]
$$

> where we used the WSS + uncorrelated assumptions: $E\{F N^{\ast}\} = E\{N F^{\ast}\} = 0$ and the PSD definitions $S_{f} = E\{FF^{\ast}\}$, $S_{\eta} = E\{NN^{\ast}\}$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Orthogonality Principle

Setting $\dfrac{\partial e^{2}}{\partial W^{\ast}(u,v)} = 0$ for the optimal $W$:

$$
-H(u,v)S_{f}(u,v) + W(u,v)\big[\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)\big] = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $W(u,v)$

$$
W(u,v)\,\big[\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)\big] = H(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}\,S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Equivalent form used in textbooks** — divide numerator and denominator by $S_{f}(u,v)$:

$$
W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v) / S_{f}(u,v)}
$$

> This is the form most commonly cited because it explicitly shows the **noise-to-signal PSD ratio** that controls the noise suppression term.

### 3.2 Minimum MSE Achieved by the Wiener Filter

The minimum mean square error equals:

$$
e^{2}_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1}\dfrac{S_{f}(u,v)\,S_{\eta}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)}
$$

> This shows that MSE vanishes only when $S_{\eta}(u,v) = 0$ (no noise) **or** $S_{f}(u,v) = 0$ at that frequency.

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Wiener Filter on a 1D-Like Degradation

> Suppose $H(u,v) = 0.5$ (constant), $S_{f}(u,v) = 4$, $S_{\eta}(u,v) = 1$. Find the Wiener filter.

**Solution:**

$$
W = \dfrac{0.5\,\cdot\,4}{(0.5)^{2}\,\cdot\,4 + 1} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 1} = 1.0
$$

Interpretation: the filter **amplifies** the noisy observation by 1.0× because the signal power is high.

> Now try $S_{\eta}(u,v) = 16$ (heavy noise):

$$
W = \dfrac{0.5\,\cdot\,4}{(0.5)^{2}\,\cdot\,4 + 16} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 16} = \dfrac{2}{17} \approx 0.118
$$

Interpretation: the filter **strongly attenuates** the noisy data because the noise dominates the signal at this frequency.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Compression Ratio & Redundancy

> A $1024 \times 1024$, 8-bit grayscale image ($n_{1} = 8 \times 1024^{2} = 8\,388\,608$ bits) is compressed to $2\,097\,152$ bits.

**Step 1 — Compression ratio:**

$$
C_{R} = \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}} = \dfrac{8\,388\,608}{2\,097\,152} = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Relative redundancy:**

$$
R_{D} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{R}} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 \quad (\text{i.e., } 75\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** 75% of the original data was redundant; the compressed representation preserves 25% of the original bit-budget and is still a complete encoding.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Entropy & Coding Redundancy

> A 3-symbol source has $P = \{0.5, 0.3, 0.2\}$. Compute (a) entropy, (b) average length with a Huffman code $l = \{1, 2, 2\}$.

**Solution (a) — Entropy:**

$$
H = -[0.5\log_{2} 0.5 + 0.3\log_{2} 0.3 + 0.2\log_{2} 0.2]
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
H = -[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.3 \cdot (-1.737) + 0.2 \cdot (-2.322)]
$$

$$
H = -[-0.5 - 0.5211 - 0.4644] = 1.4855 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Solution (b) — Average length:**

$$
L_{\text{avg}} = (1)(0.5) + (2)(0.3) + (2)(0.2) = 0.5 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Coding redundancy:**

$$
\eta_{c} = L_{\text{avg}} - H = 1.5 - 1.4855 = 0.0145 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{H}{L_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{1.4855}{1.5} = 0.9903 = 99.03\%
$$

> The Huffman code is highly efficient — only 1% short of the theoretical limit.

### 3.6 Worked Example — PSNR Calculation

> Original $f$ and compressed $\hat{f}$ have MSE = 64. Peak intensity $L = 255$. Compute PSNR.

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{64}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{65025}{64}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1016.02) = 30.07 \text{ dB}
$$

> 30 dB is the typical threshold for "perceptually acceptable" lossy compression.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wiener Filter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Image f x y] --> B[Degradation Block h x y]
    B --> C[Additive Noise n x y]
    C --> D[Degraded Image g x y]
    D --> E[2D DFT]
    E --> F[G of u v]
    F --> G[Wiener Filter W of u v]
    H[Degradation H of u v] --> G
    I[Image PSD S f of u v] --> G
    J[Noise PSD S eta of u v] --> G
    G --> K[Estimated F hat of u v]
    K --> L[2D Inverse DFT]
    L --> M[Restored Image f hat x y]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The observed spectrum $G(u,v)$ is multiplied by $W(u,v)$, which is itself constructed from $H(u,v)$, $S_{f}(u,v)$, and $S_{\eta}(u,v)$. The result is inverse-DFT'd to obtain the restored image.

## 4.2 Wiener Filter Internal Computational Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Input H u v] --> S1[Compute H star of u v]
    P1 --> S2[Compute magnitude squared H of u v]
    P2[Input S f u v] --> S3
    P3[Input S eta u v] --> S3
    S2 --> S3[Form denominator D]
    S3 --> S4[Form ratio R = S eta / S f]
    S1 --> S5[Form W = H star / D + R]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Output W of u v]
```

## 4.3 Image Compression — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Image f x y] --> B[Mapper Transform]
    B --> C[Quantizer]
    C --> D[Symbol Encoder]
    D --> E[Compressed Bitstream]
    E --> F[Symbol Decoder]
    F --> G[Inverse Mapper]
    G --> H[Output Image f hat x y]
    I[Quantizer Symbol Set] --> C
    I --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Quantizer** block is the source of **lossy** information loss (intentional irreversibility). Removing the quantizer block yields a **lossless** compression system. The **Mapper** and **Symbol Encoder** are themselves lossless (reversible).

## 4.4 Data Redundancy Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Image Data Redundancy] --> C1[Coding Redundancy]
    R --> C2[Interpixel Spatial Redundancy]
    R --> C3[Psycho Visual Redundancy]
    C1 --> C1a[Fixed length codes for non uniform sources]
    C2 --> C2a[Pixel correlation in 2D neighborhoods]
    C3 --> C3a[Human eye cannot perceive all color intensity changes]
    C3a --> C3b[Exploited by quantization]
```

## 4.5 Compression System Model — Encoder / Decoder

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Encoder
        E1[Source Image] --> E2[Mapper]
        E2 --> E3[Quantizer]
        E3 --> E4[Symbol Encoder]
        E4 --> E5[Channel / Storage]
    end
    subgraph Decoder
        E5 --> D1[Symbol Decoder]
        D1 --> D2[Inverse Mapper]
        D2 --> D3[Reconstructed Image]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> In **lossless** compression, the **Quantizer** is removed and the Mapper and Symbol Encoder are both perfectly reversible. The **compression ratio** equals the inverse of the **average codeword length** scaled by the source entropy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Wiener filter. State its key property.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Wiener filter** is a frequency-domain linear filter used in image restoration. It is formally defined as the **MSE-optimal stationary linear filter** for restoring an image $f(x,y)$ from its degraded version $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$.

Its transfer function in the frequency domain is:

$$
W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v)/S_{f}(u,v)}
$$

**Key property:** It **minimises the mean square error** $E\{[f-\hat{f}]^{2}\}$ between the original and the restored image. It gracefully degrades into a pure inverse filter at high SNR and into a noise-suppression-only filter at low SNR.

---

### **Q2. What is data redundancy? List and briefly define the three fundamental redundancies exploited by image compression algorithms.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Data redundancy** is the condition in which an image representation uses more bits than the strictly necessary minimum (i.e., the entropy bound).

**Three fundamental redundancies:**

1. **Coding Redundancy ($\eta_{c}$):** Arises when symbol probabilities are non-uniform but the code assigns uniform-length codewords. Quantified as $L_{\text{avg}} - H$.
2. **Interpixel (Spatial) Redundancy ($\eta_{s}$):** Statistical dependence between neighbouring pixel values in a 2D image.
3. **Psychovisual Redundancy ($\eta_{pv}$):** Information that the human visual system cannot perceive, which can be discarded without visible degradation (e.g., imperceptible colour differences).

**Compression ratio:** $C_{R} = n_{1}/n_{2}$. **Relative redundancy:** $R_{D} = 1 - 1/C_{R}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Wiener Filter Derivation + Implementation**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the linear degradation model $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$, derive the **Wiener filter** transfer function in the frequency domain, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **practical (parameterised) Wiener filter** form, why it is preferred when the noise and image PSDs are unknown, and compute $W(u,v)$ for the case $H(u,v) = 0.4$, $S_{f}(u,v) = 9$, $S_{\eta}(u,v) = 1$. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Degradation model in spatial domain:**

$$
g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)
$$

**Step 2 — DFT both sides:**

$$
G(u,v) = H(u,v)\,F(u,v) + N(u,v)
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate formulation:**

We seek $W(u,v)$ such that $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)G(u,v)$ minimises:

$$
e^{2} = E\{[F(u,v) - W(u,v)G(u,v)]^{2}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions:**

1. $f$ and $\eta$ are **wide-sense stationary** (WSS) random processes.
2. $f$ and $\eta$ are **uncorrelated**: $E\{F N^{\ast}\} = 0$.
3. Both have **zero mean**.

**Step 5 — Expand MSE and apply orthogonality:**

Setting $\partial e^{2}/\partial W^{\ast} = 0$ leads to:

$$
W(u,v)\,[\,\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)\,] = H(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$
\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)\,S_{f}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} S_{f}(u,v) + S_{\eta}(u,v)}\,}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $S_{f}(u,v)$:

$$
W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + S_{\eta}(u,v)/S_{f}(u,v)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Practical Wiener filter** (when $S_{f}$ and $S_{\eta}$ are unknown):

$$
W(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}\cdot\dfrac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}
$$

where $K$ is a **user-defined noise-to-signal ratio scalar** typically tuned interactively.

**Why preferred:** It avoids explicit PSD estimation, which is statistically unreliable for short image sequences. Only the degradation function $H$ and a tunable scalar $K$ are required.

**Numerical computation:**

Given $H(u,v) = 0.4$, $S_{f} = 9$, $S_{\eta} = 1$:

$$
W = \dfrac{0.4 \cdot 9}{(0.4)^{2}\cdot 9 + 1} = \dfrac{3.6}{1.44 + 1} = \dfrac{3.6}{2.44} \approx 1.475
$$

**Limiting cases:**

- $S_{\eta} \to 0 \Rightarrow W \to 1/H$ — pure inverse filter.
- $S_{f} \to 0 \Rightarrow W \to 0$ — filter rejects the frequency.
- $H = 1$, only noise: $W = S_{f}/(S_{f}+S_{\eta})$ — pure noise reduction.

---

### **Question B — Image Compression: Need for Compression**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **three fundamental data redundancies** exploited by image compression. Derive expressions for the **compression ratio** $C_{R}$ and **relative data redundancy** $R_{D}$, and compute these for an image that shrinks from $5\,\text{MB}$ to $800\,\text{KB}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grayscale source has symbol probabilities $P = \{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$ and codeword lengths $l = \{1, 2, 3, 3\}$. Compute the **source entropy** $H$, the **average codeword length** $L_{\text{avg}}$, the **coding efficiency** $\eta$, and the **coding redundancy** $\eta_{c}$. Comment on whether the code is optimal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Three redundancies:**

1. **Coding redundancy:** Sub-optimal symbol-to-codeword mapping; uniform-length codes for non-uniform sources.
2. **Inter-pixel redundancy:** Neighbouring pixels exhibit high statistical correlation.
3. **Psychovisual redundancy:** Information imperceptible to the human visual system.

**Compression ratio and relative redundancy:**

$$
C_{R} = \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}, \quad R_{D} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_{R}}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

$$
n_{1} = 5 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 41\,943\,040 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
n_{2} = 800 \times 1024 \times 8 = 6\,553\,600 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
C_{R} = \dfrac{41\,943\,040}{6\,553\,600} = 6.4
$$

$$
R_{D} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{6.4} = 1 - 0.15625 = 0.84375 \;\; (84.375\%)
$$

> The compressed image uses only ~15.6% of the original bit budget; 84.375% of the original data was redundant.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Entropy:**

$$
H = -[0.4\log_{2}0.4 + 0.3\log_{2}0.3 + 0.2\log_{2}0.2 + 0.1\log_{2}0.1]
$$

Compute each term:

$$
H = -[0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) + 0.3 \cdot (-1.7370) + 0.2 \cdot (-2.3219) + 0.1 \cdot (-3.3219)]
$$

$$
H = -[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322] = 1.8465 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 2 — Average length:**

$$
L_{\text{avg}} = (1)(0.4) + (2)(0.3) + (3)(0.2) + (3)(0.1) = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.9 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{H}{L_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{1.8465}{1.9} = 0.9719 = 97.19\%
$$

**Step 4 — Coding redundancy:**

$$
\eta_{c} = L_{\text{avg}} - H = 1.9 - 1.8465 = 0.0535 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Comment:** The code is **near-optimal**, achieving 97.19% efficiency. The remaining 2.81% is irreducible coding redundancy (due to integer-length constraints of prefix codes). A Huffman construction would have produced this exact code.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Wiener Filter Questions**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the **complex conjugate** $H^{\ast}(u,v)$ — students write $H$ instead. This is correct **only** if $H$ is purely real.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the **inverse filter** $1/H(u,v)$ with the **Wiener filter**. Always check whether the question mentions noise — the Wiener filter is the noise-aware form.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_{2}$ in entropy — KTU expects **base-2** (bits) when the symbol is "bits/symbol".
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to specify the **WSS, uncorrelated, zero-mean** assumptions in the Wiener filter derivation. These carry **1.5 marks** by themselves.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In compression-ratio problems, students often forget to convert KB to bits (×8) and use $1024$ vs $1000$ inconsistently. KTU uses $1\,\text{KB} = 1024$ bytes unless stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Wiener Filter**

- [x] Degradation model: $g = h \ast f + \eta \;\Rightarrow\; G = HF + N$
- [x] Wiener filter formula (must memorise both forms):

$$
W = \dfrac{H^{\ast}\,S_{f}}{\vert H\vert^{2} S_{f} + S_{\eta}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{H}\cdot\dfrac{\vert H\vert^{2}}{\vert H\vert^{2} + K}
$$

- [x] Minimises **MSE**; reduces to **inverse filter** when $S_{\eta} \to 0$
- [x] Requires $H$, $S_{f}$, $S_{\eta}$ (or scalar $K$ in the practical form)
- [x] Behaviour: attenuates noisy frequencies gracefully, never blows up
- [x] Assumptions: WSS, uncorrelated, zero-mean

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Image Compression: Need**

- [x] Three redundancies: **Coding**, **Interpixel (Spatial)**, **Psychovisual**
- [x] Compression ratio: $C_{R} = n_{1}/n_{2}$
- [x] Relative redundancy: $R_{D} = 1 - 1/C_{R}$
- [x] Source entropy: $H = -\sum P \log_{2} P$ (bits/symbol)
- [x] Average code length: $L_{\text{avg}} = \sum l_{j} P(a_{j})$
- [x] Coding redundancy: $\eta_{c} = L_{\text{avg}} - H \geq 0$
- [x] Efficiency: $\eta = H / L_{\text{avg}} \leq 1$
- [x] Fidelity criteria: **MSE / RMSE / SNR / PSNR** (objective); **observer rating** (subjective)
- [x] Lossless pipeline = Mapper + Symbol Encoder (no quantiser)
- [x] Lossy pipeline = Mapper + Quantiser + Symbol Encoder (quantiser is irreversible)
- [x] **Shannon bound:** $L_{\text{avg}} \geq H$, equality only with perfect code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for Exam:**
> *"Wiener is **smart inverse**: it knows about noise and image power; compression is **redundancy removal**: coding + spatial + psychovisual."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Lossless Compression and Lossy Compression. Concept of Transform Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT755 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Lossless Compression and Lossy Compression. Concept of Transform Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Image Compression?

**Image Compression** is the art and science of reducing the amount of data required to represent an image. It is a fundamental operation in digital image processing that enables efficient storage and transmission of visual information across bandwidth-limited channels.

Formally, image compression is the process of minimizing the size in **bits** of a digital image without degrading the visual quality below an acceptable threshold.

Mathematically, the goal is to achieve a high **Compression Ratio (CR)**:

$$CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$$

where $n_1$ is the number of bits in the original image and $n_2$ is the number of bits in the compressed image.

A related metric is the **Relative Data Redundancy (R)**:

$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{CR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Image compression addresses the problem of reducing the amount of data required to represent a digital image. The underlying premise is that images contain significant amounts of **redundancy** — spatial redundancy, spectral redundancy, and psycho-visual redundancy — which can be removed without destroying essential information.

### Types of Redundancy in Digital Images

1. **Coding Redundancy:** Occurs when the code words used to represent gray levels are longer than absolutely necessary. Fixed-length codes (like 8-bit per pixel) are inefficient when pixel probabilities vary widely.

2. **Spatial (Inter-pixel) Redundancy:** Adjacent pixels in most images are highly correlated. A pixel value can often be predicted from its neighbors.

3. **Spectral (Inter-band) Redundancy:** In color/multispectral images, different spectral bands exhibit high correlation.

4. **Psycho-visual Redundancy:** The human visual system does not respond with equal sensitivity to all visual information. Certain information is psychologically less important and can be eliminated.

---

## 1.2 Lossless Compression (Reversible Compression)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Lossless compression is a class of data compression algorithms that allow the **exact original image** to be reconstructed from the compressed data. No information is permanently lost. The reconstructed image after decompression is mathematically identical (bit-for-bit) to the original.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Folding Suitcase

Imagine packing clothes for travel. You carefully fold each shirt into a tight, structured pattern — rolling, layering, optimizing space. When you arrive at your destination, you can **unfold every shirt back to its original shape**. Nothing is destroyed; only the representation was reorganized for efficiency.

Similarly, **lossless compression** transforms image data into a more compact representation using clever statistical or dictionary-based encoding, but guarantees **perfect reconstruction**. This is critical for medical imaging, satellite imagery, and archival storage where every pixel matters.

### Common Lossless Techniques

- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** — Exploits sequences of identical values.
- **Huffman Coding** — Uses variable-length codes based on symbol probability.
- **Arithmetic Coding** — Encodes entire messages as a single number in $[0,1]$.
- **LZW (Lempel-Ziv-Welch)** — Dictionary-based substitution coding.
- **Bit-Plane Coding** — Decomposes image into binary bit planes.
